notes.text: Preserve every movement: compassion and the four thousand (1-10), the demanded sign (11-13), bread/leaven and failed memory (14-21), sight in two stages at Bethsaida (22-26), Peter's confession (27-30), Peter's resistance to the suffering path (31-33), and costly discipleship (34-38). Partial-to-clear sight may guide the chapter, but it must not flatten it.

The opening likely continues the broader Decapolis setting from Mark 7, but Mark 8 does not name the feeding site or say the crowd is entirely Gentile. Provision reveals Jesus' compassion; it does not promise every material shortage resolves on our timetable. Do not manufacture symbolic codes from the crowd, loaf, fish, or basket numbers.

The Pharisees' demand is a testing demand after abundant signs; do not imply that honest questions or careful evidence-seeking are sinful. Pharisees are Jewish interlocutors, not priests by definition, foreign outsiders, or visual villains.

The leaven of the Pharisees and Herod is a spreading influence, but its exact content is debated. Do not make Pharisees and Herodian supporters identical, and do not reduce the boat scene to the disciples being stupid: they are holding remembered evidence and still struggling to perceive what it means.

Preserve the staged healing exactly: Jesus leads the man outside Bethsaida, partial sight follows the first action, and clear sight follows the second. Mark does not explain why. Never describe Jesus as failing, experimenting, losing power, or being surprised; label its connection to the disciples' partial sight as strong literary inference.

Build Peter's confession from Mark alone. It occurs on the road among the villages around Caesarea Philippi, not explicitly at the Pan grotto, a pagan temple, a famous cliff, or Mount Hermon. Peter has the right title but resists Jesus' stated path; unstated expectations and motives remain interpretation.

Jesus' rebuke identifies Peter's counsel as adversarial to God's way; it does not say Peter is demon-possessed. Keep the sequence of suffering, rejection, death, and resurrection. Peter's acceptance of Jesus' identity alongside his resistance to Jesus' path is an interpretive contrast, not permission to invent his unstated expectations.

Mark 8:35 centers costly allegiance to Jesus and the good news, including real loss. A false self trying to be its own lord may be secondary application, not the verse's whole meaning. Cross-bearing never sanctifies abuse, coercion, burnout, self-harm, preventable danger, self-neglect, or suffering-seeking, and never tells someone to remain with an abuser.

Map only responsible regions: probable eastern/Decapolis context, unidentified Dalmanutha, debated Bethsaida, and the villages around Caesarea Philippi. Where evidence supports a most-probable location, show a labeled probable point inside a translucent uncertainty area; where it does not, show only the area. Never imply a verified route. Images remain people-first and must not merge separate moments or import parallel-Gospel details.

Image candidates should distinguish the chapter's movements rather than repeat a feeding tableau: the seven baskets after the crowd has eaten can carry the evidence-and-memory theme; a restrained subjective partial-sight treatment may illuminate the staged healing; and a Peter-rebuke scene should retain the other disciples because Jesus turns and sees them. Never place the remembered baskets in the boat, depict Satan as a visible figure, or portray Peter as possessed. These are candidates, not mandatory image slots; Selah still chooses the strongest chapter-shaped set.

Preserve the full movement: the hinge saying (1), Transfiguration (2-8), descent and Elijah (9-13), suffering boy and father (14-29), passion prediction (30-32), greatness and the child (33-37), the person outside the Twelve's circle (38-41), and stumbling, radical holiness, salt, and peace (42-50). Explain how the mountain scene and the following failed-ministry scene interpret one another without collapsing either.

The Transfiguration's placement makes it a strong interpretive foretaste of 9:1, but whether it exhausts the saying is debated. Mark does not name the mountain. Tabor is traditional; a northern/Hermon-region proposal follows the preceding geography. Moses-and-Elijah meanings, Peter's shelter symbolism, and exact visual extent are interpretation; show no humanoid Father, halo, glowing eyes, beams, or identifiable mountain landmark.

Mark describes symptoms that are seizure-like to a modern reader and explicitly identifies a spirit in this particular story. Do not diagnose epilepsy retrospectively, generalize seizures, disability, or mental illness as demonic, blame the boy or father, displace medical care, or use convulsion as visual spectacle. Compassion and restored dignity come before explanation.

The father's mixed faith is brought honestly to Jesus and met without shame. Treat faith as potentially genuine even when emotionally quiet, divided, or plainly expressed; write this insight freshly rather than reproducing a study-chat line. Do not promise this episode's timing or outcome to every suffering family. Continued suffering is never proof of deficient faith.

Prayer in 9:29 means dependence on God, not a technique, power ranking, correct phrase, or guaranteed method. Some manuscripts add fasting; treat it as an edition-specific textual note. Do not invent a taxonomy behind 'this kind.'

Hold glory and the passion prediction together: Jesus' glory is revealed in willing, cross-shaped love. Suffering is not inherently holy, and this text never licenses abuse, burnout, self-neglect, avoidable harm, or seeking pain.

In Capernaum, Jesus answers a status argument with a child. Children could be loved while lacking adult rank and power; do not call them worthless. The child's age, gender, household, and identity are unknown. Prefer a child standing beside or within Jesus' arm rather than a default lap pose.

The unnamed person acting in Jesus' name corrects possessive gatekeeping, not all discernment. Do not call him an unbeliever, heretic, rival leader, or secret apostle. The disciples do not own Jesus' work or get to equate their circle with the whole kingdom.

Hand, foot, and eye removal is severe rhetorical hyperbole demanding decisive resistance to sin, never bodily injury. Apply it to access, secrecy, habits, environments, lies, or systems. Gehenna should not be called a perpetually burning first-century city dump. Salt-and-fire wording and meaning are difficult and must remain transparent; the clear landing is faithful distinctiveness and peace.

Keep separate scenes separate: the suffering boy, the child in the greatness lesson, and the millstone warning are not one child or one staged moment. Map Galilee and Capernaum with an unnamed mountain and unknown route. Prefer the restored-by-the-hand human moment as hero over medical spectacle or superhero Transfiguration imagery.

Preserve the full road-to-Jerusalem movement: divorce and hard hearts (1-12), children received (13-16), the wealthy man and divine possibility (17-31), passion prediction (32-34), James and John and servant greatness (35-45), and Bartimaeus receiving mercy and following on the way (46-52). Track how the scenes test attempts to secure kingdom life through legal control, status, possessions, rank, or crowd power, while Jesus centers receiving, service, and following.

Jesus returns the divorce test from legal permission to God's joining intent and human hardness. Mark 10 is not a complete modern pastoral policy and does not explicitly address abuse. Never use it to trap someone in danger, minimize harm, force unsafe reunion, or make a generated draft decide complex pastoral care.

Children are textually welcomed and blessed; Mark does not define them as morally innocent, identify all caregivers as mothers, or give exact ages. Dependence, receptivity, and low social rank are responsible interpretations, not direct definitions.

In Mark-local copy call him the wealthy man. Matthew supplies 'young' and Luke supplies 'ruler.' Do not import a youthful face, official title, signet, guards, colors, or authority symbols. Jesus' look and love, the man's fallen response, grief, many possessions, and departure are explicit; his sincerity, greed, inner monologue, later fate, and permanent rejection are unknown.

Do not weaken the wealth warning into a claim that money is neutral whenever one's attitude is good, and do not invent the later Needle Gate legend. The direct command is to this man; Mark's broad warning remains intentionally unsettling. Human salvation is impossible as an achievement and possible with God; do not turn the scene into a prosperity or poverty formula.

Jesus walks ahead and names rejection, death, and resurrection. His forward position and repeated prediction support a narrative/theological synthesis of willing movement toward suffering, but not a camera-level claim about every private thought. The disciples' amazement and fear are explicit; exact route and expressions remain reconstruction.

James and John ask for honor immediately after the passion prediction. Do not make them cartoonishly selfish or assume the other ten's indignation is purer. Kingdom greatness takes the shape of service because Jesus' mission takes the shape of costly self-giving; 'ransom for many' must not be forced to carry every detail of one atonement theory.

Bartimaeus is named, calls for mercy, receives sight, and follows on the way. Mark creates a strong contrast with status-seeking, but does not say he fully understands every part of Jesus' mission. Portray disability with dignity: no fabricated diagnosis, white or clouded eyes, helpless spectacle, or claim that his cloak was his only possession or proves total surrender.

Historic map: broad Judea and trans-Jordan context, Jordan Valley, Jericho region, and Jerusalem as destination; dashed possible movement only. Do not pin teaching scenes or the healing roadside. Modern borders must not be projected backward into first-century regions.

Images remain people-first: the wealthy man's observable grief under Jesus' loving attention; varied children and caregivers; Jesus walking ahead; servant-greatness correction without thrones or foot washing; Bartimaeus called into the center. No divorce tableau, Needle Gate, synoptic identity imports, or invented interior motives.

Preserve Mark's three-day narrative sequence: entry, late Temple inspection, and return to Bethany (1-11); next-day fig tree (12-14) and Temple action (15-19); following-morning withered tree, prayer and forgiveness teaching (20-26), and authority challenge (27-33). Do not label Sunday, Monday, or Tuesday as text-explicit.

Mark names one unridden young animal, cloaks, and leafy material cut from fields. A donkey is highly probable from Zechariah and parallel accounts, but Mark's noun is broader. Mark does not identify palms, waving, the two disciples, prior arrangement, the entry gate, crowd size, or the weekday. Do not say this is the same crowd later involved in Jesus' death.

Jesus first looks around the Temple and leaves because it is late; the next day's action is narratively measured, not an arrival tantrum. The fig tree is leafy, fruitless, and explicitly out of season. Mark's interwoven tree and Temple structure strongly invites interpretation, but a reading of fruitless worship or Temple order under judgment must remain labeled and must never become a curse on Jewish people or ethnic Israel. Do not solve the season tension with folklore or depict an instant special effect.

Mark places Jesus in the broad Temple precinct. The Court of the Gentiles is a plausible reconstruction, not explicit. Exchange and animal sales served legitimate pilgrimage and sacrifice needs; Mark alleges no dishonest rate. Use 'Temple action' before explaining judgment or cleansing. No whip, injuries, rage claim, violent assault, inner sanctuary, or movie brawl.

Read Jesus' quotations in their prophetic contexts: Isaiah 56 centers prayer and inclusion of peoples otherwise excluded; Jeremiah 7 condemns injustice, violence, idolatry, and using sacred space as refuge from accountability. The den-of-robbers image is larger than retail price-gouging. Jesus, disciples, leaders, merchants, and crowd are Jewish; name the particular groups Mark names and reject anti-Jewish caricature or replacement theology.

The withered tree is discovered the following morning; Peter notices it. Jesus directs faith toward God, not confidence in confidence. The mountain saying may be rhetorical impossibility, Mount of Olives and Temple context, or another referent; do not settle it. Never promise health, wealth, or any request on demand, or blame unanswered prayer and suffering on weak faith.

Forgiveness does not mean calling harm good, waiving accountability, restoring trust or access automatically, reconciling without repentance, or remaining in danger. A prayer can release vengeance to God while keeping truth, justice, boundaries, and safety.

Mark 11:26 is a genuine textual and numbering variant: important earlier witnesses omit the additional sentence while later manuscripts include a parallel to Matthew. Follow the licensed reader edition and explain calmly; verse 25 already contains Mark's forgiveness teaching.

The authority challenge names chief priests, teachers of the Law, and elders. Their recorded deliberation makes the answer strategic, but do not invent additional private motives, name Caiaphas, call it a formal Sanhedrin session, add Pharisees, Sadducees, or Roman soldiers, or make leaders racially distinct villains. A theological synthesis may locate Jesus' authority in Mark's broader presentation of God's Son, but must be labeled rather than presented as a camera-level fact of this exchange.

Map Bethany, the Mount of Olives ridge, Jerusalem and the Temple, and approximate Bethphage or a representative approach; no exact road, gate, fig tree, or vendor court. Images: humble entry without palm-pageant harmonization; quiet Temple inspection; restrained Temple action plus teaching; withered-tree discovery; controlled authority confrontation. Use the Herodian and Second Temple world, with no modern Dome, Solomon's Temple, pseudo-readable sacred text, whip, gore, caricature, or identical mob.

comparison_rubric: Mark-local facts with labeled synoptic context
text, inference, interpretation, and unknown discipline
Selah's wise-friend cadence and memorable natural lines
Jesus-centered theological depth without forced connections
historical, geographic, medical, disability, and pastoral integrity
chapter-shaped prayer and application without promises, blame, or harm
no licensed Bible text in generated output
no copied private reference or exemplar wording
